title: Learning to Discover Regulatory Elements for Gene Expression Prediction

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Seq2Exp, a framework for predicting gene expression from long DNA sequences and epigenomic signals by learning a sparse, position-wise mask intended to capture actively interacting regulatory elements. The mask distribution is modeled as a product-of-experts over two Beta distributions—one conditioned on sequence via a neural network and one conditioned on signals via a non-parametric mapping—under a conditional independence assumption, and is trained via an information bottleneck objective. Experiments on two ENCODE cell lines (K562, GM12878) show improved CAGE prediction over sequence-only long-context baselines, a signals-augmented Caduceus baseline, and EPInformer; the learned masks are also reported to outperform MACS3 peak calls when used to select regions.
strengths: The product-of-experts formulation over Beta distributions to combine sequence- and signal-derived mask probabilities is an interesting, simple-to-implement idea that yields a tractable family under multiplication.
Framing region selection as a learned, differentiable token-level mask with an information bottleneck prior is appealing and builds on rationale extraction literature with a genomics-oriented twist.
Includes strong long-sequence baselines (Enformer, HyenaDNA, Mamba, Caduceus) and a multimodal baseline (EPInformer), plus an informative “Caduceus w/ signals” control.
Uses a rigorous chromosome-based split including chrX, which is a reasonably tough test bed.
The high-level motivation, architecture overview, and training pipeline are clearly described; the use of reparameterization for Beta sampling is well motivated.
Tables report multiple metrics (MSE, MAE, Pearson), which helps triangulate performance differences.
Learning to identify functional regions that causally drive gene expression is an important and timely goal in computational genomics.
The framework, if robustly validated, could be a generally useful mechanism for integrating modalities and producing interpretable, controllably sparse attributions.
weaknesses: The core factorization p(m|X) ∝ p(m|Xseq)p(m|Xsig) is derived by dropping the prior p(m) in p(m|X) ∝ p(m|Xseq)p(m|Xsig)/p(m). This is not generally valid unless p(m) is constant w.r.t. m (which a Beta prior is not). As written, Proposition 1 conflicts with the derivation in Appendix A.1, and the role of the prior is internally inconsistent (it is both dropped in the factorization and used in the KL term).

The conditional independence assumption p(Xsig, Xseq|M)=p(Xsig|M)p(Xseq|M) is strong and biologically questionable: epigenomic signal amplitudes often correlate with motif content and sequence context beyond a binary “selected/not-selected” region mask. No empirical test of this assumption (e.g., conditional independence checks) is provided.

The signals mapping α2 = Xsig; β2 = Cβ is underspecified and potentially ill-posed. Beta shape parameters must be strictly positive and are sensitive to scale; multiple modalities (d > 1) raise dimension-mismatch questions; without normalization or a positive mapping (e.g., softplus) the construction may produce invalid or degenerate posteriors.

Missing key baselines: recent multimodal approaches such as Prism (2602.21550) that explicitly target confounding in epigenomic integration and report stronger results than Seq2Exp-soft on the same K562/GM12878 CAGE task and protocol. GraphReg is discussed but not included experimentally.
Limited scope: only two cell lines are evaluated; no tests of cross-cell-type transfer or robustness to modality removal/noise; no uncertainty estimates or multiple seeds.

Ablations are thin: there is no systematic dissection of (i) the impact of the product-of-experts vs. a single learned gating network; (ii) the role of each modality in α2; (iii) sensitivity to the sparsity prior (α3,β3); (iv) soft vs. hard masking beyond headline metrics; or (v) mask threshold Cm and β weight in the IB loss.

The MACS3 comparison does not control for mask ratio; q-value/thresholds in MACS3 can be tuned to match the learned mask fraction for a fair comparison. Only a 10% “retrain” variant is shown on Seq2Exp; MACS3 uses 13–16%.
Ambiguity around how multi-track signals are combined into α2 per base; how signals are preprocessed, scaled, and guaranteed positive; and how Hi-C (binned) is aligned to single-base Beta parameters.

The claim that combining two Betas “results in another Beta” should explicitly note normalization constants; while the kernel is Beta, care is needed to ensure valid, normalized densities in implementation.
Some causal language is overstated relative to identification: the method is predictive with regularization; no interventional tests or robustness analyses support causal claims.

Beyond Prism, recent diagnostic work (2606.06834) on interpretability emphasizes controlling for predictability biases; the paper’s interpretability/region-discovery claims would benefit from such controls and from overlap analyses with ENCODE cCREs or CRISPR-validated enhancers.
Broader benchmarking efforts (e.g., GENEB 2606.04525) highlight instability of aggregate leaderboards; stronger positioning relative to category-specific tasks and evaluation variance would help.
reviews.questions:
1. In Appendix A.1, why is p(m) dropped in the decomposition p(m|X) ∝ p(m|Xseq)p(m|Xsig)/p(m)? Since p(m) is a function of m, this alters the shape unless the prior is uniform. How is this reconciled with the Beta prior used in the KL term?
2. How are multi-track signals (DNase, H3K27ac, Hi-C-derived ABC scores) combined to form α2 per base? What preprocessing and positivity constraints are applied to ensure valid Beta parameters and reasonable dynamic ranges?
3. Can you provide ablations isolating: (a) product-of-experts vs. a single learned gating network; (b) the contribution of each modality in α2; (c) sensitivity to (α3,β3), the β weight in the IB loss, and the hard/soft threshold Cm?
4. How stable are results across random seeds, and are the reported gains statistically significant (e.g., CIs over multiple runs)?
5. Can you add comparisons to Prism and GraphReg under the same splits and features? If not feasible, can you contextualize your SOTA claim in light of reported Prism results that outperform Seq2Exp-soft on the same task?
6. For the MACS3 comparison, can you tune MACS3 thresholds to match mask ratios (e.g., 6–10%) and report performance as a function of mask fraction for both methods? Also, please report overlaps of learned masks with ENCODE cCREs and validated enhancers.
7. Did you examine how much the predictor relies on signals vs. sequence when masks are applied? For example, how does performance change if signals are withheld at test time, or if signal modalities are perturbed/noised?
8. How is Hi-C information, typically at 5 kb resolution, aligned to single-base parameters in α2, and does this mismatch affect learning?
9. Can you empirically assess the conditional independence assumption p(Xsig, Xseq|M)=p(Xsig|M)p(Xseq|M) (e.g., residual correlations after conditioning on sampled masks)?
10. What is the computational cost (wall-clock/GPU-hours) relative to the “Caduceus w/ signals” baseline, and how does inference latency scale with mask sparsity?
reviews.assessment: This paper tackles an important and active problem—integrating DNA sequence and epigenomic signals to both predict gene expression and localize regulatory elements—and introduces a neat product-of-experts Beta masking idea combined with an information bottleneck. Empirically, the model performs well against strong long-sequence baselines and EPInformer on two ENCODE cell lines, and the learned masks appear to select informative regions. However, I have substantive concerns about the theoretical justification of the mask factorization (dropping the prior in the decomposition), the strength and untested nature of the conditional independence assumption, and ambiguities in the mapping from multimodal signals to Beta parameters. Experimentally, the scope is limited (two cell lines, no seed variance), key recent baselines (e.g., Prism) are missing, and interpretability claims rely solely on predictive performance without orthogonal biological validation or confounder controls. Overall, the idea is promising and likely valuable with further development, but the current submission falls short of top-tier standards in methodological rigor and comparative evaluation. I recommend rejection at this stage, with a clear path to strengthening via corrected derivations, expanded ablations, matched-ratio peak comparisons and biological overlap analyses, and inclusion of more recent multimodal baselines.
